---
title: "Game of Thrones: Viewership Report"
author: "Meifang Wu 473459"
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    number-sections: true
    theme: cosmo
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

```{r}
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(knitr)
library(scales)

got <- data.frame(
  season = 1:8,
  episodes = c(10, 10, 10, 10, 10, 10, 7, 6),
  avg_viewers_m = c(2.52, 3.80, 4.97, 6.84, 6.88, 7.69, 10.26, 11.99)
)

got <- got %>%
  mutate(
    change_m = avg_viewers_m - lag(avg_viewers_m),
    pct_change = 100 * change_m / lag(avg_viewers_m)
  )

summary_tbl <- data.frame(
  Statistic = c(
    "Network",
    "Number of seasons",
    "Number of episodes",
    "First air date",
    "Last air date"
  ),
  Value = c(
    "HBO",
    "8",
    "73",
    "2011-04-17",
    "2019-05-19"
  )
)

peak_season <- got %>% slice_max(avg_viewers_m, n = 1)
largest_jump <- got %>% filter(!is.na(change_m)) %>% slice_max(change_m, n = 1)
smallest_jump <- got %>% filter(!is.na(change_m)) %>% slice_min(change_m, n = 1)
```

# Introduction

*Game of Thrones* is an American fantasy drama television series created for HBO. The show ran for eight seasons and became one of the most well-known television series of the 2010s. In this report, I examine how its average U.S. viewership changed across seasons.

# Image

![](images/Game_of_Thrones_title_card.jpg){fig-align="center" width="60%"}

# Basic Statistics

```{r}
kable(summary_tbl, caption = "Basic information about Game of Thrones")
```

# Season-Level Viewership Data

```{r}
display_tbl <- got %>%
  mutate(
    `Season` = season,
    `Episodes` = episodes,
    `Average viewers (millions)` = round(avg_viewers_m, 2),
    `Change from previous season` = ifelse(
      is.na(change_m), "-", round(change_m, 2)
    ),
    `Percentage change` = ifelse(
      is.na(pct_change), "-", paste0(round(pct_change, 2), "%")
    )
  ) %>%
  select(
    `Season`,
    `Episodes`,
    `Average viewers (millions)`,
    `Change from previous season`,
    `Percentage change`
  )

kable(display_tbl, caption = "Season-level viewership statistics")
```

# Viewership Over Time

```{r}
ggplot(got, aes(x = season, y = avg_viewers_m)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 1:8) +
  labs(
    title = "Average U.S. Viewership by Season",
    x = "Season",
    y = "Average viewers (millions)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)
```

# Season-to-Season Change

```{r}
ggplot(got %>% filter(!is.na(change_m)), aes(x = season, y = change_m)) +
  geom_col() +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 2:8) +
  labs(
    title = "Season-to-Season Change in Average Viewership",
    x = "Season",
    y = "Change in viewers (millions)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)
```

# Discussion

The results show a clear upward trend in the average U.S. viewership of *Game of Thrones*. The average audience increased from `r got$avg_viewers_m[1]` million viewers in season 1 to `r got$avg_viewers_m[8]` million viewers in season 8.

The highest average viewership was recorded in season `r peak_season$season`, with `r peak_season$avg_viewers_m` million viewers. The largest increase occurred between season `r largest_jump$season - 1` and season `r largest_jump$season`, when average viewership rose by `r round(largest_jump$change_m, 2)` million viewers.

By contrast, the smallest increase happened between season `r smallest_jump$season - 1` and season `r smallest_jump$season`, when average viewership increased by only `r round(smallest_jump$change_m, 2)` million viewers. Overall, the show gained `r round(got$avg_viewers_m[8] - got$avg_viewers_m[1], 2)` million average viewers from the first season to the last season.

These patterns suggest that the series became increasingly popular over time, especially in its later seasons. Although the growth was not equally strong every year, the general trend was clearly positive.

# Conclusion

This report analyzed the season-level viewership of *Game of Thrones*. Based on the available data, the show experienced substantial audience growth across its eight seasons. Average U.S. viewership increased from 2.52 million in season 1 to 11.99 million in season 8. Overall, the results suggest that the series attracted a much larger audience in its later seasons than at the beginning.
